item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s an excellent way to describe the flaws of the GOC. A harmless chair turned into a dangerous pile of scraps all because the GOC decided to put it through a wood chipper. Great job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why the fuck does a chair make me sad
can we fix this chair?

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A perfect example of why we stan the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another one here who loves this piece. It's the perfect length and for how short that length is it still manages to come full circle, and I am moved. This is great, economical writing. The containment procedure is one of the most original and intriguing I've encountered.

Truly, a 'contained' article…. eheheh ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one so much!

For starters I should say that I basically never like SCPs that involve "GOI"s. I think SCP works best as a collection of disconnected, standalone articles- so things like reoccurring characters or groups really turns of off. but this article is one example where the author did things exactly right. Anyways, now that that's out of the way…

The article jumps out at readers immediately by doing what few SCPs can manage: making a really interesting containment procedure. As soon as you read that, your natural reaction is confusion and a desire to learn more.

The rest of the article is just so original and clever, while still remaining simple. And by the end I was actually feeling strangely emotional for a chair, which is a really odd sensation. I just can't say enough good about this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I can see why people see the note as being too on the nose it makes perfect sense in-universe. When writing a narrative it's better not to beat the reader over the head with your point but the Foundation doctor isn't writing a narrative, he's justifying an organisation-wide policy. In that case it behoves him to be very clear and direct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like a child seeking protection…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the reasons why I hate the GOC. No hate on the guy who wrote about them in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least it's not SCP-1730, possibly the biggest, nastiest example of why one shouldn't try to indiscriminately destroy every anomaly they come across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've taken to listening to TheVolgun's SCP readings for background noise while at work, and this one left enough of an impression on me to seek it out and upvote it. I love the concept and execution both. I grant that the end message does hammer the point home a bit much, but it seems like the right choice 'in-character', as I can absolutely see this as the Foundation's go-to counterpoint when talking about their mission vs. the GOC's. I may just have a soft spot for it because I loved how TheVolgun read it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is why we're right and the GOC is wrong, people.

This line is my big problem with this SCP — it's way too ham-fisted and lacking in subtlety. The end note is already getting close to that, and this just pushes it right over the top with an explicit statement of the moral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coming back to reread this SCP after years, and I still think the note is just right. It's the SCP foundation choosing to emphasize its own best interpretation of its motivations. There are so many cases of barely-defensible protocols that they're involved in, aiming to contain the worst things… they kind of need this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly how I see it. And it works amazingly well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized, after seeing fanart of this on Reddit, that containment of this thing basically boils down to hugging it and saying "Shhhhhh. It's okay. We still love you. The bad men can't hurt you anymore."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Necro, but I like Scantron's interpretation that this is mostly a propaganda vehicle for the Foundation. "See? This is what happens when you just blow shit up willy-nilly!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, which provides amazing reasoning as to why the Foundation are the real good guys. It shows the GOC in an incompetent fashion which reflefcts the policies of the GOC, who try and destroy scips before understanding them.

This SCP perfectly sums up the GOC's "good intentions"; no different from the Axis Powers they originated from… can't help but feel sad for this dear lady.
.
You got me feeling bad for a murderous mass of wood: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could probably put it back together if you had someone really good at jigsaw puzzles, but I doubt it would have enough integrity for the thing to hold together under a person's weight unless you used something like a huge amount of epoxy, to the point where it was more epoxy than wood.

The real problem, though, is that it's been traumatized- letting it be mulch gives it a calm, quiet way to be useful, which is what it really wants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello Mr. Mulch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, I was wonder why can't it just be glued back together and just have a nice chair. But maybe it is better as mulch.

*Yes I know that the comment is from 2012 but I kind of agreed with the person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: RIP Mr. Chair

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very scary prank would be to build a chair like the image in somebody's house while they are away and leave it by the front door! What would you do if you came home and there was a humanoid armchair staring back at you? Or there was a humanoid armchair in your room when you woke up and it was talking to you?

item.title: Image
item.content:encoded: Where did the image come from? I am curious to know who would come up with this weird chair design!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was planning on making a joke about an "armchair psychiatrist", and then I thought about the article a little more, and now I genuinely want such a thing to exist in-universe.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor chair. Its like dog that got hurt for trying to be playful.

item.title: I like this one.
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but empathize with this SCP. It just wanted to be helpful, and it gets fed into a freaking woodchipper for its troubles because people don't understand it. No good deed goes unpunished, and I've honestly had experiences like that myself in dealing with people.

I hope it feels loved and appreciated now. Upvoted.

/What a lovely flowerbed! I've never seen such healthy flowers before!
//It must be due to this amazing mulch! This garden would never flourish so much without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Prior to the Coalition getting their hands on this, it was perfectly harmless. A chair which teleports to you when you need a seat is normal compared to most of the stuff that we deal with on a regular basis. When they put it through a woodchipper, it got hurt, scared and angry, so it lashed out at them. By trying to 'protect the world' by destroying it, they inadvertently made the situation a whole lot worse.

It's kinda like killing a mockingbird in a way

#worsecomparisonever

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is the exact wrong way to get across the "contain not destroy" message. In effect, it's a horrid strawman.

There was no way of knowing that this char would be sentient/hostile if/when destroyed. Add to this that even when it was not broken it did a minor anomalous thing and didn't hurt anyone or anything or leak radiation into another dimension, there would be no real pressing need to destroy this anyway. Since the GOC seems to be all about destroying anomalous things, that might be a given that they would try and destroy it anyway, but for the Foundation or anyone else, there's simply no pressing need to destroy the thing pre-GOC or any reason not to just let it play.

The problem of whether or not to destroy certain anomalies is a delicate issue and there are good reasons to go about doing so. The example set here is a badly put-together strawman that basically amounts to "Never destroy anything because something might backfire and go wrong and we have no way of knowing what so don't even bother throwing anything away". That basically amounts to hoarder-type behavior. And this isn't a case of "Foundation ARE hoarders" because that's NOT what a hoarder is. The Foundation is a scientific/research outfit that seeks to study and understand anomalies at best, and at worst to prevent anomalies from being publicly exposed and doing damage. A hoarder keeps things because they cannot bear to throw them away. They keep things they don't need and it does damage to them and/or their house and family.

The ethics of "containment vs destruction" is something that would better be explored with a violent or potentially dangerous/paradigm-shifting anomaly, for which there is a justifiable reason to destroy beyond "it's weird-lookin", not for something like this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's alright as is. The author might have been trying to avoid starting two sentences the same way.

Edit: I saw your edit, sandlionman. It's been reverted. Both changes are stylistic and up to the author to decide; please do not make such fixes without authorial and/or staff permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When the page says "It is to be used as mulch within this flowerbed." Is that supposed to mean that SCP-1609 is used as mulch or something else. If it means SCP-1609 should be used as mulch I suggest changing the sentence to "SCP-1609 is to be used as mulch within this flowerbed."

item.title: Re: Make it into modular building blocks to increased usefulness?
item.content:encoded: The Foundation don't know how well they can communicate with it, they just know it seems to understand the preservative procedure (a procedure that was extremely carefully road-tested to ensure the minimum possible amount of danger). They don't even know how intelligent this thing is (it actually isn't very intelligent at all, by human standards - it's rather like a dog in a lot of respects). And if they mess up the procedure, it could kill people, and they might never be able to re-contain it. On a risk/reward basis, there's not much to be gained by improving its usefulness, because what they have works, and changing it could potentially open up a very dangerous can of worms.

item.title: Make it into modular building blocks to increased usefulness?
item.content:encoded:
Since the preserving products seem to be incorporated into it (no mention of it leaving shells of stuff behind when teleporting), whatever binding agent is used in the reprocessing would probably work as well.

So why don't they offer it the choice of being pulverized, mixed into a binding agent and placed into molds to turn the splinters and other chunks into modular building blocks, to allow it to make itself even more useful?

Though, it would probably be a good idea to suggest it to first try providing just a very small splinter to the process, to confirm if it would indeed remain intact after the process, and of course to verify if the process isn't too unpleasant as well.

And to avoid loss of life though, it might be necessary to communicate the offer via a teleoperated robot, and perhaps have the the grinder device have a spring-loaded weight
-activated power button, to allow for 1609 to be fully in control of the process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems it wants to be useful; so if it is something that is more decorative than useful it might not react so well. Maybe if a little girl made the point about the doll house it might accept it; but it would be a dangerous thing to try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't risk further trauma.
But hypothetically speaking, what would the odds be of 914 restoring the chair?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a good example of the "reminder that we dont destroy things" scp's. Ive seen some that just sound preachy when they say it, but this one seemed smooth and inclusive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite liked the initial "hook" given by the picture of the chair combined with the immediate description of "SCP-1609 is, at the present time, a pile of splinters, wood chippings…" — was a nice "oh shit, what happened?" moment. The resulting story failed to disappoint, so +1'd for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hesitated before +1ing because it does not demonstrate enough on the focal point, which is the emphasis on containing and protecting anomalous shtuff instead of destroying them. All we have here is a chair that started to kill people because it is hurt by the GOC, I sort of wanted more from this article.

But then, it's a damned splinter pile that teleports into people's lungs. Awesome! +1

item.title: Great
item.content:encoded: Now THIS is the way to show "Secure, Contain, Protect" is not "Destroy, Destroy, DESTROY!". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Threat ID: KTE-0937-Velveteen "Sixth Chair."

…

History: Initially one of a set of six matching objects originally constructed by KTE-7201-Pygmalion ("The Carpenter")

…

KTE-0937-Velveteen was disposed of by maceration after a mechanical failure in the incinerator device. Artifact proceeded to escape through teleportation, and has since been responsible for the deaths of several agents. Believed to currently be in the possession of The Foundation.

…

Addendum: KTE-0937-Velveteen is an object lesson in the importance of following proper operating procedure. Due to the lack of vigilance by the agent on the scene, the object's threat level was escalated, the object itself was not successfully disposed of, and it has since fallen into the hands of a hostile agency. A single failure by a single operative resulted in the deaths of six. Remember this the next time you think about cutting corners.
— Assistant Director "Kipling"

Taken from the GOC wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the note at the end acts like containment doesn't lead to mistakes you can't take back. I think this works best as a look at how the Foundation filters information for its members.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do rather enjoy that this shows why The Foundation rarely destroys objects.
Why didn't I upvote this sooner?

item.title: Upvoted
item.content:encoded:
I loved that note at the end. It represents the soul of the SCP foundation. If I was to introduce someone to the concepts of this site, I think I'd introduce them to this page along with a few other classics, such as the classic concrete monster we all know and love.

The rest of the article was great, too. A benevolent anomaly, broken by malicious humans, now incredibly dangerous and nigh-indestructible… and now the Foundation has to deal with what's left. And it does so with great success in spite of the fact that its job was made harder by the more malicious organizations.

item.title: Re: People, the note is awesome.
item.content:encoded:
"Because destroying everything is not the solution. Now you know it, and knowing is half the battle!" <theme song>

Ok, I liked the article, but the note is a bit self-indulgent.

item.title: People, the note is awesome.
item.content:encoded: I love this and I even love the note. It makes the whole aesop of the article evident rather than requiring those of us who are bad with subtlety to guess it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw man, this SCP makes me sad. Poor magic chair just wanted to be a chair. Now it's not a chair anymore…

Makes me wonder how well it would respond to being picked up/re purposed into something new, though. Like a cute little dollhouse full of tiny furniture, or something…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i cry evertim
;_;7

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document at the end is such poetic justice, makes me like the Foundation even more. Cold as it may be, at least it isn't making things worse. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the note at the end because it makes perfect sense from an in-universe perspective. Makes the article as a whole seem more alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They'd still have the records, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor thing.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Well, that didn't last for very long. Someone I actually know removed their upvote so it couldn't reach +100.

EDIT: …And now it's back up. I'm still amazed this was recieved so well - I was over the moon when I found out it had reached +70, since I never thought I'd get something that high.
'gratz man!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lovely object lesson.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somebody apparently copied this over to the GOC wiki, expanding a bit on the history of the artefact and looking at the situation from the GOC's perspective. I approve heartily.

Here it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for great justice.

I would recommend perhaps a note saying that they're planning on repairing the item in future to reduce the potential danger of a containment breach, but the SCP's still a bit too traumatised and untrusting to fully cooperate.

Though as a propaganda piece, they might not want to fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, hard. I hope this gets a +100, it deserves it/
